narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes a solid sixth-decile ranking in your first-year school grades, which is slightly above average, but a third-decile ranking in your third-year grades, well below the typical fifth-decile level; an undergraduate GPA of 3.00 compared to the average 3.21; an LSAT score of 32, lower than the usual 36.7; upper-middle family income; and plans to attend part-time, which is less common as most students go full-time. The model predicted a 50% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, and because law schools set a high bar for admission, this tipped the scales toward rejection to prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your third-year decile ranking, as lower performance there strongly signals potential struggles on the bar. On a more positive note, your choice to attend part-time actually nudged the prediction toward success, countering some concerns since part-timers often demonstrate strong commitment despite outside responsibilities. Your first-year decile and undergraduate GPA also helped by pushing slightly toward a passing prediction, showing early promise and decent overall academics. Even your LSAT, while below average, had a minor pull toward failure, but it wasn't the dominant influence. Compared to typical students—who boast higher third-year rankings, stronger LSATs, and full-time plans—your profile stood out in ways that raised risks in the model's eyes, especially that dip in later-year performance. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law; it's just one data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year academics through targeted prep or additional courses, and exploring full-time options if feasible—these steps could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. Keep pushing; many have overcome similar hurdles to pass the bar and thrive. We're rooting for you.
